title: Apply: Voting, Rebels, Debt, and Congressional Power
description: Apply your knowledge about sections two to five of the Fourteenth Amendment to this set of your multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Fourteenth Amendment: Citizenship, Freedom, and Equality
Voting, Rebels, Debt, and Congressional Power
Apply: Voting, Rebels, Debt, and Congressional Power

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The cartoon shows a man sleeping in a hammock. The man is labeled "Congress" and the hammock is labeled "law enforcement." An elephant in the background says "Don't wake him up!" to a donkey. In the foreground, a broken blunderbuss, which is type of gun, lies on the ground. The blunderbuss is labeled "14th Amendment, 2nd Section." In the lower left-hand corner, a black boy with a drum around his neck looks out sadly.
options.caption: “Congress – 14th Amendment 2nd Section” by Edward Windsor Kemble, 1902

choices.content: Congress fails to uphold the part of the Fourteenth Amendment that denies states representation if they deny Black voters their rights.
Congress’s failure to ratify the Fourteenth Amendment has led to the violation of citizenship rights for Black people.
Congress enforces the Fourteenth Amendment to increase its own powers at the expense of Black Americans.
Congress has spent too much time and money empowering law enforcement to police the actions of Black Americans.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the significance of Section 2 of the Fourteenth Amendment being portrayed as a broken gun?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The cartoon shows a man sleeping in a hammock. The man is labeled "Congress" and the hammock is labeled "law enforcement." An elephant in the background says "Don't wake him up!" to a donkey. In the foreground, a broken blunderbuss, which is type of gun, lies on the ground. The blunderbuss is labeled "14th Amendment, 2nd Section." In the lower left-hand corner, a black boy with a drum around his neck looks out sadly.
options.caption: “Congress – 14th Amendment 2nd Section” by Edward Windsor Kemble, 1902

choices.content: It shows that the Constitution doesn’t grant the government enough power to force states to protect the rights of Black people.
It shows that the government is not using the power it has to enforce Section 2 of the Fourteenth Amendment.
It shows that the government is too focused on enforcing the Second Amendment, rather than the Fourteenth Amendment.
It shows the government’s lack of authority to pass legislation that protects individual rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Daniel Clark’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866. Clark is discussing the proposed Fourteenth Amendment**

[L]ooking toward the exclusion of many of those who participated in the rebellion from participation in the administration of our Government . . . . I much prefer that you should take the leaders of the rebellion, the heads of it, and say to them, “You never shall have anything to do with this Government” and let those who have moved in [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] return to their loyalty and to the Government.

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment would most directly address the concerns that Senator Clark expresses in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: activities; areas over which someone has authority

choices.content: Section 1
Section 2
Section 3
Section 4

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Daniel Clark’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866. Clark is discussing the proposed Fourteenth Amendment**

[L]ooking toward the exclusion of many of those who participated in the rebellion from participation in the administration of our Government . . . . I much prefer that you should take the leaders of the rebellion, the heads of it, and say to them, “You never shall have anything to do with this Government” and let those who have moved in [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] return to their loyalty and to the Government.

**Which statement best summarizes Clark’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: activities; areas over which someone has authority

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment should focus on preventing the main leaders of the rebellion from serving in government.
The Fourteenth Amendment should not bar any person from serving in government, even if they joined the rebellion.
The Fourteenth Amendment should bar all participants in the Confederacy from holding office, as they all rebelled against the government.
The Fourteenth Amendment should require all new members of Congress to swear their loyalty to the government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

I do not believe the people of the United States will object to declaring that the whole of the rebel debt shall be eternally [[☃ definition 1]] and extinguished—a debt contracted in the [[☃ definition 2]] of the most wicked war with which the earth was cursed, against a Government that was never felt by them except in the benefits it [[☃ definition 3]]. Such a debt can never be assumed or repaid by the  loyal people of the United States, and if [[☃ definition 4]] to remain in [[☃ definition 5]] existence it can only be left in that condition as a subject of political squabbling and party [[☃ definition 6]].

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment would address the concerns that Senator Howard expresses in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Section 1
Section 2
Section 3
Section 4

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

I do not believe the people of the United States will object to declaring that the whole of the rebel debt shall be eternally [[☃ definition 1]] and extinguished—a debt contracted in the [[☃ definition 2]] of the most wicked war with which the earth was cursed, against a Government that was never felt by them except in the benefits it [[☃ definition 3]]. Such a debt can never be assumed or repaid by the  loyal people of the United States, and if [[☃ definition 4]] to remain in [[☃ definition 5]] existence it can only be left in that condition as a subject of political squabbling and party [[☃ definition 6]].

**Which statement best summarizes Howard’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: If the former Confederate states do not pay their debt, the division within the United States will never heal.
No person should be taxed to pay for the war debt, as such taxation would be a violation of their rights.
The Confederate war debt should be canceled because loyal Americans should not be required to pay off debts that the Confederates incurred while rebelling.
If the government fails to pay the debt of the Confederate states, those states will never recover after the Civil War.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

Now, sir, there is no power given in the Constitution to enforce and to carry out any of these [[☃ definition 1]]. They are not powers granted by the Constitution to Congress, and of course do not come within the sweeping clause of the Constitution authorizing Congress to pass all laws necessary and proper for carrying out the [[☃ definition 2]] or granted powers, but they stand simply as a bill of rights in the Constitution, without power on the part of Congress to give them full effect; while at the same time the States are not restrained from violating the principles embraced in them except by their own local constitutions, which may be altered from year to year. 

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment would most directly address Senator Howard's concerns?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the rights listed in the first eight amendments

choices.content: Section 2
Section 3
Section 4
Section 5

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

Now, sir, there is no power given in the Constitution to enforce and to carry out any of these [[☃ definition 1]]. They are not powers granted by the Constitution to Congress, and of course do not come within the sweeping clause of the Constitution authorizing Congress to pass all laws necessary and proper for carrying out the [[☃ definition 2]] or granted powers, but they stand simply as a bill of rights in the Constitution, without power on the part of Congress to give them full effect; while at the same time the States are not restrained from violating the principles embraced in them except by their own local constitutions, which may be altered from year to year. 

**Which statement best summarizes Howard’s argument in this excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the rights listed in the first eight amendments

choices.content: Although the Constitution gives Congress authority over state governments, it does not authorize Congress to pass any laws.
Although the Constitution lists certain rights, it doesn’t give Congress the power to protect those rights from state violations.
Although the Constitution is the supreme law of the land, state governments should be able pass laws restricting rights listed in the Constitution.
Although the Constitution lists some rights, certain unnamed rights are not protected by any level of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866. Bingham is discussing the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

Mr. Speaker, there is another section which simply prohibits the United States or any State of this Union from ever assuming or paying any part of the rebel debt or making [[☃ definition 1]] from emancipated slaves. I do not believe that there is a man on this floor who can answer to his [[☃ definition 2]] for withholding his vote from that proposition. It involves the future [[☃ definition 3]] of the nation. It is a declaration in solemn form, if accepted by the people, that the resources of this great country shall be used in the future, not to [[☃ definition 4]] debts contracted in aid of rebellion, not to pay for emancipated slaves, but to maintain [[☃ definition 5]] the [[☃ definition 6]] faith of the nation to all world and especially to its dead and its living defenders.

**Which statement best summarizes Bingham’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the people an elected official represents

choices.content: Every representative should support this provision, as it upholds the nation's honor by ensuring that the resources of the U.S. won't be used to cover Confederate debts.
Every representative should vote to remove this provision, as it will not support the rebuilding efforts in the South or help Black Americans adjust to life after emancipation.
Every representative should support this provision to pay off the debts of the former Confederacy, as doing so will strengthen the bond between northern and southern states.
Every representative should vote to remove this provision to allow the southern states to rejoin the Union without paying their debts, as it does not effectively punish the South.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866. Bingham is discussing the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

Mr. Speaker, there is another section which simply prohibits the United States or any State of this Union from ever assuming or paying any part of the rebel debt or making [[☃ definition 1]] from emancipated slaves. I do not believe that there is a man on this floor who can answer to his [[☃ definition 2]] for withholding his vote from that proposition. It involves the future [[☃ definition 3]] of the nation. It is a declaration in solemn form, if accepted by the people, that the resources of this great country shall be used in the future, not to [[☃ definition 4]] debts contracted in aid of rebellion, not to pay for emancipated slaves, but to maintain [[☃ definition 5]] the [[☃ definition 6]] faith of the nation to all world and especially to its dead and its living defenders.

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment is the author referring to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the people an elected official represents

choices.content: Section 2
Section 3
Section 4
Section 5

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Thaddeus Stevens’s speech in the Senate in 1866. Stevens is speaking about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

The second section I consider the most important in the article. It fixes the basis of representation in Congress. If any State shall exclude any of [[☃ definition 1]] adult male citizens from the elective [[☃ definition 2]] or abridge that right, she shall forfeit her right to representation in the same proportion. The effect of this provision will be either to [[☃ definition 3]] the States to grant universal suffrage or so to [[☃ definition 4]] their power, so as to keep them forever in a hopeless minority in the national Government, both legislative and executive. 

If they do not enfranchise the freedmen, it would give to the rebel States but thirty-seven Representatives. Thus shorn of their power, they would soon become [[☃ definition 5]]. Southern pride would not long [[☃ definition 6]] a hopeless minority. True it will take two, three, possibly five years before they conquer their prejudices sufficiently to allow their [[☃ definition 7]] slaves to become their equals at the polls. . . . Do not tell me that there are loyal representatives waiting for admission—until their States are loyal they can have no standing here. They would merely misrepresent their constituents.

**Which statement best summarizes Stevens’s argument about the second section of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: that is, the state’s; throughout this speech, Stevens refers to states as “she” and “her”

choices.content: It fails to accomplish its goal of extending the right to vote to Black men, as states still regulate voting rights.
It will force states to allow Black men to vote; otherwise, the states will lose political power in the federal government.
It will fail to accomplish its goal of re-admitting southern states to the Union, as they will refuse to give Black men the right to vote.
It will lead to Black men taking their rightful place in the federal government as members of Congress.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania Representative Martin Russell Thayer’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866. Thayer is discussing the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

I am opposed to the [third section of the amendment] because it looks to me like offering to the people of the States lately in rebellion peace and restoration with one hand, while you snatch it from them with the other. I am opposed to it because I think it will keep this country, which we seek to [[☃ definition 1]] and to bring back to its old state of allegiance, in a state of constant turmoil and [[☃ definition 2]] if it does not rekindle afresh the fires of civil war.

**Which statement best summarizes Thayer’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: cause to be peaceful and not angry

choices.content: The extension of citizenship to free Black people falls short, because the amendment does not do enough to secure the voting rights of Black citizens.
The federal government’s refusal to take on the Civil War debt of the former Confederacy will lead those states to economic disaster.
The federal power to enforce the Fourteenth Amendment will destroy the balance of powers between the federal and state governments.
The disqualification of former Confederates from holding office is unfair and will cause continued tension between North and South.
